question: Derrida's concept of 'différance' applied to Caribbean text
options: The return of repressed colonial trauma as horror
Caribbean identity always defers fixed meaning, always differing from itself
Uses humour, irony and social commentary drawn from calypso traditions
The generative power of the spoken word to create reality
answer: Caribbean identity always defers fixed meaning, always differing from itself

question: The concept of 'archive fever' (Derrida) in Caribbean writing
options: Caribbean identity always defers fixed meaning, always differing from itself
The obsessive desire to recover and preserve what colonialism destroyed
Defining non-Western cultures as inferior to construct Western superiority
Writing the body and experience outside patriarchal language structures
answer: The obsessive desire to recover and preserve what colonialism destroyed

question: The concept of 'rememory' (Morrison) applies to Caribbean writing through
options: Examines who has the power to look and how looking constructs the other
The way traumatic history persists in the present
Resisting fixed identities and embracing movement and becoming
Uses humour, irony and social commentary drawn from calypso traditions
answer: The way traumatic history persists in the present

options: Examines who has the power to look and how looking constructs the other
Resisting fixed identities and embracing movement and becoming
Uses humour, irony and social commentary drawn from calypso traditions
Internalising the coloniser's values and seeing oneself as inferior
answer: Internalising the coloniser's values and seeing oneself as inferior

answer: Using Caribbean folk and popular culture as the basis for literary analysis

answer: Writing the body and experience outside patriarchal language structures
